title: Swap index driver to better-sqlite3 when Bun/native toolchain support is available
Core Types & Utilities
description: Shared type definitions, token estimation, path normalization, and deterministic helpers. These are the leaf-level utilities that every other component imports. Zero external component dependencies — only stdlib + chosen libraries. This layer defines the 'vocabulary' of the system: what a Selection looks like, what a Priority is, how we estimate tokens, how we generate stable run IDs. Getting these right early avoids churn later.

title: Shared type definitions for all components
close_reason: Completed: added shared type contracts and exports in src/types

title: Token estimation utility
description: Implement a fast token estimation function that approximates token counts without requiring a real tokenizer. Strategy: use a character-based heuristic (characters / 3.5 for English/code, adjustable ratio) that's fast enough to run on every file. This is deliberately approximate — we don't need BPE accuracy for budget enforcement, just consistent estimates. The function should handle: raw text estimation, file content estimation (reading from disk), and selection-level estimation (sum of all selected content). Must be deterministic — same input always gives same estimate. The ratio should be configurable but stable across runs. Consider: code has more whitespace and short identifiers than prose, so the ratio may differ by language. For v1, a single ratio is acceptable; we can refine later if budget accuracy proves insufficient.

title: Path normalization utilities
description:
Utilities for consistent path handling throughout the system. Functions needed:

- normalizePath(path, repoRoot): convert to relative path from repo root, normalize separators
- toAbsolute(relPath, repoRoot): convert relative to absolute
- isSubpath(candidate, parent): check if candidate is under parent (no symlink escape)
- pathDisplay(path, mode: 'relative'|'absolute', repoRoot): format for display
- matchGlob(path, pattern): test if a path matches a glob pattern (using micromatch or similar)

Key invariant: all paths stored internally are relative to repo root, using forward slashes. Display formatting is applied only at output time. This prevents the 'same file, different path' problem that would break determinism. Path normalization is one of the most common sources of subtle bugs in tools like this — getting it right early saves enormous pain.

title: Deterministic helpers (stable sort, hash, run ID)
description:
Utility functions for maintaining determinism across runs:

- stableSort<T>(items, compareFn): sort that guarantees stable ordering (Array.sort is not guaranteed stable in all runtimes, though modern engines do this)
- stableHash(content: string): content-addressable hash (SHA-256 truncated to 12 hex chars) for cache keys and file identity
- generateRunId(repoRoot, timestamp): deterministic run ID = ISO timestamp + '-' + short hash of repo root. Example: '20240115T143022-a1b2c3d4'. Used for artifact storage paths.
- truncateStable(text, maxChars, ellipsis): truncate text at a character boundary, always append the same ellipsis marker

These are small but critical — without stable sort and stable hash, two runs of the same input could produce different outputs, violating ctx's core guarantee.

title: Index Manager
description: SQLite-backed persistent index for file metadata, symbols, and imports. The index enables fast lookups for discovery without re-scanning the filesystem every run. Schema stores: file path, size, mtime, content hash, detected language, extracted symbols (from codemap), and import/export relationships (best-effort). Key design decision: incremental updates by comparing mtime+hash, so warm runs skip unchanged files. The index also stores schema version for automatic rebuild on schema changes. This is optional (--no-index skips it) but dramatically improves warm-start performance.

title: SQLite schema design and initialization
description:
Design and implement the SQLite schema for the persistent file index. Tables:

files: id, path (unique), size, mtime, content_hash, language, line_count, indexed_at
symbols: id, file_id (FK), kind (function|class|interface|type|export|const), name, signature, line_number, end_line
imports: id, file_id (FK), imported_path, imported_names (JSON array), is_reexport
schema_meta: key, value (stores schema_version for migration detection)

Indexes: files(path), symbols(file_id), symbols(name), imports(file_id), imports(imported_path).

Use better-sqlite3 (synchronous, fast, battle-tested with bun). The schema_meta table stores the current schema version; if it doesn't match the code's expected version and config.index.rebuild_on_schema_change is true, drop and recreate all tables. Database file location: .ctx/index.db (repo-local) or config.cache_dir. Initialize on first use; no separate migration step needed for v1.

title: File metadata indexing
description: Index file metadata from the scanner's FileEntry[] into SQLite. For each file: upsert into the files table with path, size, mtime, content_hash, detected language. Language detection uses file extension mapping (a comprehensive map: .ts→typescript, .tsx→typescriptreact, .js→javascript, .py→python, .rs→rust, .go→go, .java→java, .rb→ruby, .c→c, .cpp→cpp, .h→c, .hpp→cpp, .cs→csharp, .swift→swift, .kt→kotlin, .md→markdown, .json→json, .yaml→yaml, .toml→toml, etc.). Batch inserts for performance (use transactions — wrap entire index update in a single transaction). Track indexed_at timestamp for staleness detection.

title: Incremental index update logic
description: Only re-index files that have changed since last index. Detection: compare mtime from stat() against stored mtime. If mtime differs, recompute content_hash. If hash also differs, re-index the file (update metadata, re-extract symbols and imports). If mtime is the same, skip entirely. Also handle deletions: files in the index but not in the current scan should be removed from the index. This is critical for warm-start performance — a 10k-file repo where 5 files changed should take <1s to update, not 15s to re-scan everything. Implementation: (1) load all indexed paths+mtimes into a Map, (2) for each scanned file, check against map, (3) batch-insert changed files, (4) delete removed files.

title: Import/export extraction (best-effort)
description:
Extract import/export relationships from source files for import graph proximity scoring. This is BEST-EFFORT — it does not need to be perfect, just useful for ranking. Strategy: regex-based extraction for common patterns:

TypeScript/JavaScript: import ... from 'path', require('path'), export ... from 'path'
Python: import module, from module import name
Go: import "path"
Rust: use crate::path, mod name

Parse the imported path and resolve it to a file path in the repo (best-effort: try common resolution rules like adding extensions, checking index files). Store in the imports table: file_id, imported_path (as resolved relative path), imported_names (JSON array of imported symbols). Don't try to be a full-fidelity module resolver — that's a rabbit hole. Just capture the most common patterns. This data feeds into the priority scoring algorithm for import graph proximity.

title: CLI Frontend & Pipeline
description: The CLI frontend handles argument parsing, command routing, stdin/file task input, output routing (stdout/file/clipboard), stderr progress/warnings, exit codes, and — most importantly — the main pipeline orchestration that ties all components together. The pipeline is: parse args → load config → scan repo → update index → run discovery → enforce selection/budget → assemble prompt → write artifacts → output. Each step has clear error handling and fallback behavior. The CLI must feel snappy: progress on stderr, prompt on stdout, clean exit codes.

title: Argument parser (all flags and subcommands)
description:
Implement CLI argument parsing for all flags defined in the spec. Use a parsing library (e.g., commander, yargs, or citty — prefer something lightweight that works well with bun). Define:

MAIN COMMAND: ctx [TASK_TEXT] [flags]
SUBCOMMANDS: init, agents, index [--rebuild], templates (list|show <name>), explain (last|<run-id>), manifest (last|<run-id>) [-o <path>], open (last|<run-id>)

FLAGS (grouped):
- Mode/output: --mode, --format, -o/--output, --copy/--clipboard, --quiet, --verbose, --json-summary
- Budget: --budget, --reserve, --max-files, --max-full-files, --max-slices-per-file, --max-file-bytes, --fail-on-overbudget
- Discovery: --discover, --agent, --model, --agent-timeout, --agent-max-turns, --no-llm, --dry-run
- Repo: --repo, --cache, --cache-dir, --no-index
- Context: --tree, --codemaps, --line-numbers
- Includes: --include, --exclude, --prefer-full, --prefer-slices, --prefer-codemap, --entrypoint (all repeatable)
- Git: --diff, --git-status, --git-max-files, --git-max-patch-tokens
- Privacy: --privacy, --redact, --redact-pattern, --never-include (repeatable)

Validate: mutually exclusive flags (--no-llm and --discover llm), required args for subcommands. Exit code 2 for invalid usage.

title: Subcommand routing
description:
Route parsed CLI arguments to the appropriate handler:

- No subcommand + TASK_TEXT: main pipeline (runDiscovery)
- 'init': initialize .ctx/ directory structure and default config
- 'agents': show discovery agent info (available backends, configured keys)
- 'index': manage the persistent index (--rebuild for full rebuild, default for status)
- 'templates list': list available templates (built-in + custom)
- 'templates show <name>': display a template's content
- 'explain last|<id>': show explanation for a run
- 'manifest last|<id>': output manifest JSON for a run
- 'open last|<id>': open generated prompt in $PAGER

Each handler is a separate function that receives the parsed config and performs its action. Unknown subcommands: exit code 2 with usage hint. The main pipeline handler is by far the most complex — it's the pipeline orchestration (separate task).

title: Stdin and file task input
description:
Support multiple ways to provide the task text:

1. Positional argument: ctx 'Add OAuth login' (most common)
2. Stdin pipe: cat task.md | ctx (for multiline tasks)
3. File reference: ctx --task-file task.md (read task from file)

Detection logic: if positional arg is provided, use it. Else if stdin is not a TTY (piped), read stdin. Else if --task-file is provided, read the file. Else: error 'no task provided' (exit code 2).

For stdin: read all input, trim whitespace, use as task text. For large stdin inputs (>10KB): warn that the task text is unusually large (it might be a file accidentally piped). The task text is stored verbatim in the run record for reproducibility.

title: Output routing (stdout, file, clipboard)
description:
Route the assembled prompt to the configured destination:

1. Default (stdout): write prompt to process.stdout. This is the primary mode — users pipe or paste from terminal.
2. --output <path> / -o <path>: write to a file. Create parent directories if needed. Overwrite existing file (with warning if file exists and is non-empty).
3. --copy / --clipboard: copy to system clipboard. Use platform-specific commands: pbcopy (macOS), xclip or xsel (Linux), clip (Windows). If clipboard command not available, warn and fall back to stdout.

In all cases, stderr receives progress/warnings/token report (unless --quiet). stdout NEVER contains anything except the prompt (or nothing if --output/--copy is used). This separation is critical for piping: 'ctx task | model-cli' must pipe clean prompt text without debug noise.

title: stderr progress and warning output
description:
Implement structured progress and warning output on stderr. Progress messages:

1. 'Scanning repository...' (during file walk)
2. 'Updating index...' (during incremental index update)
3. 'Discovery: using <backend> (<model>)' (backend selection)
4. 'Discovery: turn N/M...' (during LLM discovery)
5. 'Assembling prompt...' (during prompt assembly)
6. Token report summary: 'Budget: 60000 | Estimated: 45230 | Files: 42 (8 full, 15 slices, 19 codemap)'

Warnings: 'Warning: no API key configured, using offline discovery', 'Warning: N files exceeded size limit', 'Warning: budget exceeded, applied N degradations', 'Warning: redacted N secrets'.

All progress uses stderr. Controlled by --quiet (suppress all) and --verbose (add timing details). If stderr is not a TTY (piped), omit progress bars/spinners. Keep messages concise and informative — this is operator-level UX, not debug logging.

title: Pipeline orchestration (main run flow)
description:
The main pipeline that ties all components together. This is the 'conductor' function called when the user runs 'ctx <task>':

1. Parse CLI args → merged config
2. Detect repo root
3. Load/update scanner (file walk + filtering)
4. Load/update index (incremental, skip if --no-index)
5. Select discovery backend (auto-selection logic)
6. Run discovery (offline, LLM API, or local CLI)
7. Take discovery result (selection + handoff summary)
8. Apply hard constraint enforcement
9. Apply budget normalization (degradation ladder if needed)
10. Read file contents for selected files
11. Generate codemaps for codemap_only files
12. Collect git diff/status (if enabled)
13. Render all prompt sections (via section renderers)
14. Assemble final prompt (via template engine + format variant)
15. Write artifacts (run record, manifest)
16. Output prompt (stdout/file/clipboard)
17. Output token report summary (stderr)

Error handling at each step: log warning, attempt recovery, fall back if possible. The pipeline should almost always produce output — only fail hard on truly unrecoverable errors (no repo, no readable files). Performance: measure elapsed time per step for the run record.

title: Testing completeness initiative: realism-first coverage + full e2e diagnostics
description:
Goal: close the testing gap for realism and operational confidence.

Observed gaps (current state):
- Test suite uses many injected fakes/stubs/mocked runtime call sites in unit tests.
- E2E coverage exists but is not a complete scenario matrix and lacks rich structured logging artifacts for diagnosis.
- No explicit policy defining where fakes are prohibited vs acceptable boundary simulation.

Exit criteria:
- Policy, coverage map, and implementation tasks completed.
- Real-behavior coverage expanded for high-risk modules.
- E2E matrix includes success/failure paths with deterministic, high-detail logs.
- CI enforces the agreed thresholds and publishes diagnostics on failures.

comments.author: Neil Pattanaik
comments.text:
A1 inventory complete: fake/stub/mocked seam classification baseline

## Summary
Current test suite is broad, but **not no-fake**. We have concentrated seam injection in subprocess boundaries, provider adapter boundaries, scanner IO overrides, and CLI runtime harness capture.

### Inventory counts (unique test files)
- Process/subprocess seam injection: 6 files
- Scanner IO override seams: 2 files
- Discovery adapter simulated transports/tool dispatch: 6 files
- CLI runtime capture harness tests: 3 files
- File IO injection seams for loader/templating paths: 5 files

## Detailed seam map

### 1) Process/subprocess seam injection (HIGH risk area)
Files:
- `test/git/runner.test.ts`
- `test/git/diff.test.ts`
- `test/git/status.test.ts`
- `test/search/ripgrep.test.ts`
- `test/tools/git-tools.test.ts`
- `test/tools/search-tools.test.ts`

Seams used:
- `spawnSyncImpl`
- `runGitCommandImpl`
- `executeDiffModeImpl`
- `searchContentImpl`, `searchPathsImpl`, `isRipgrepAvailableImpl`, `searchPathsFallbackImpl`

Classification:
- **Boundary-required** for rare process failures (ETIMEDOUT, ENOENT, malformed CLI output) that are hard to force deterministically in real env.
- **Replaceable with real behavior** for many happy-path and normal-failure paths currently tested only via injected returns.

Risk:
- High: overuse can hide true CLI/env parsing issues and subprocess contract drift.

Replacement target:
- `ctx-1az.1.3` (real temp git repos + real rg-driven coverage where feasible).

### 2) Scanner IO override seams (MEDIUM-HIGH risk area)
Files:
- `test/scanner/walker.test.ts`
- `test/scanner/gitignore.test.ts`

Seams used:
- `resolveGlobalGitignorePath`
- `readBinaryChunk`

Classification:
- Mixed: some are legitimate boundary control; some can be replaced by fixture-backed real files and temporary HOME/git config wiring.

Risk:
- Medium-high: scanner is foundational to selection quality; synthetic chunking can hide real FS edge behavior.

Replacement target:
- `ctx-1az.1.2` (expand real fixture and permission/symlink edge tests).

### 3) Discovery adapter transport simulation (MEDIUM risk area)
Files:
- `test/discovery/openai-adapter.test.ts`
- `test/discovery/anthropic-adapter.test.ts`
- `test/discovery/google-adapter.test.ts`
- `test/discovery/claude-cli-adapter.test.ts`
- `test/discovery/codex-cli-adapter.test.ts`
- `test/discovery/gemini-cli-adapter.test.ts`

Seams used:
- `runChatCompletion` / `runMessages` / `runGenerateContent` / CLI invocation delegates
- `dispatchToolCalls`
- `sleep`

Classification:
- **Boundary-required** for external provider/CLI transport and rate-limit/auth behavior.
- Still needs stronger “protocol-realistic” transcript corpus and malformed payload adversarial coverage.

Risk:
- Medium: transport mocking is expected, but protocol drift can be missed without richer transcript realism.

Replacement target:
- `ctx-1az.1.4` (protocol-realistic corpus, malformed/adversarial cases, less shortcut payloading).

### 4) CLI runtime capture harness seams (MEDIUM risk area)
Files:
- `test/cli/subcommand-routing.test.ts`
- `test/cli/golden-snapshots.test.ts`
- `test/integration/cli-e2e.test.ts`

Seams used:
- custom `CliRuntime` capture (`isStdinTty`, `readStdin`, `readFile`, `copyToClipboard`, `openInPager`)

Classification:
- Partly required for deterministic assertion of side effects.
- Partly replaceable with black-box command execution harness and structured artifact assertions.

Risk:
- Medium: runtime harness can diverge from real shell process behavior and suppress observability gaps.

Replacement target:
- Track B (`ctx-1az.2.1` → `ctx-1az.2.6`) for full e2e harness + structured logs.

### 5) File IO injection seams in artifact/template loader tests (LOW-MEDIUM)
Files:
- `test/artifacts/explain.test.ts`
- `test/artifacts/manifest.test.ts`
- `test/prompt/templates.test.ts`
- (also appears in CLI harness files above)

Classification:
- Mostly acceptable for parser/formatter unit granularity.
- Add targeted real-file counterparts where loader behavior can vary by platform.

Risk:
- Low-medium.

Replacement target:
- Fold into `ctx-1az.1.2` / `ctx-1az.1.4` as scoped follow-ups.

## Risk-ranked priority order for seam reduction
1. HIGH: process/subprocess path realism (`git`, `rg`, tool wrappers) → `ctx-1az.1.3`
2. MEDIUM-HIGH: scanner real FS behavior (`gitignore`, binary detection, permission edges) → `ctx-1az.1.2`
3. MEDIUM: adapter protocol realism (while keeping boundary simulation) → `ctx-1az.1.4`
4. MEDIUM: CLI runtime capture to full e2e harness/logging evolution → Track B tasks
5. LOW-MEDIUM: parser/loader IO seams as opportunistic hardening

## Replacement strategy mapping (handoff)
- `ctx-1az.1.2`: real fixture-heavy scanner/index edge coverage
- `ctx-1az.1.3`: real subprocess-backed git/search test lanes + targeted synthetic-only rare error tests
- `ctx-1az.1.4`: protocol-realistic discovery corpus, malformed response cases
- `ctx-1az.1.5`: codify explicit fake-policy guardrails to prevent seam creep
- `ctx-1az.1.6`: compile module-level seam-delta and residual-risk report for closure

Track A realism readiness report (A6)

## 1) Completion status (A1-A5 dependencies)

## 2) Seam delta vs A1 baseline
A1 baseline (file-count lens) vs current scan:

| Seam category | Baseline (A1) | Current | Delta | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Process/subprocess seam files | 6 | 6 | 0 | Same seam file count, but realism increased via integration lanes and reduced fake-only assertions in runner/ripgrep lane. |
| Scanner IO override seam files | 2 | 2 | 0 | Same seam file count; real-behavior symlink/nested-ignore/index-path tests were added in A2. |

| Artifact/template IO seam files | 5 | ~5 | ~0 | Not a primary focus in A2-A4; remains lower-priority residual seam area. |

Interpretation:
- **Track A did not reduce seam-file cardinality dramatically**, but it did convert key high-risk behavior from synthetic-only assertions to real boundary execution and richer contract validation.
- This is a meaningful realism gain for git/search/scanner/discovery despite stable seam-file counts.

## 3) Module-level realism coverage evidence
High-risk modules now have strong real-behavior coverage slices:

- Git/search subprocess path:
  - Integration realism files: `test/git/diff.integration.test.ts` (10 tests), `test/git/runner.integration.test.ts` (3), `test/search/ripgrep.integration.test.ts` (3).
  - Unit seams retained mainly for forced boundary failures (timeout/spawn errors).

- Scanner/index filesystem path:
  - Existing/expanded suites include `test/scanner/walker.test.ts` (8), `test/scanner/gitignore.test.ts` (5), `test/index-manager/incremental-qa.test.ts` (7).
  - Added real symlink and nested `.gitignore` behavior checks in A2 lane.

- Discovery/tool-host protocol path:
  - Adapter suites: `test/discovery/{openai,anthropic,google,claude-cli}-adapter.test.ts` (24 total tests across these files).
  - Contract suites: `test/tools/ctx-final-validator.test.ts` (9), `test/tools/protocol-contract.test.ts` (5), `test/tools/tool-call-parser.test.ts` (4).
  - A4 hardening now validates latest corrected `ctx_final` payload and keeps parse/schema diagnostics actionable.

Validation runs used for this report:
- `bun test test/git test/search`
- `bun test test/discovery test/tools`
- `bun run build`

## 4) Remaining unavoidable fake seams and residual risk
### High / medium residuals
1. CLI runtime harness seams (MEDIUM)
- Files: `test/cli/subcommand-routing.test.ts`, `test/cli/golden-snapshots.test.ts`, `test/integration/cli-e2e.test.ts`, `test/integration/e2e-harness.ts`.
- Risk: harness behavior can drift from true shell/runtime behavior.
- Mitigation owner path: Track B (`ctx-1az.2.*`).

2. Discovery transport seam injections (MEDIUM)
- External network/provider/CLI invocation paths still require simulation for auth/rate-limit/network/timeouts.
- Risk reduced by new transcript realism + malformed corpus, but transport-layer simulation remains inherently synthetic.
- Mitigation: keep these seams only at boundary layer per A5 policy.

3. Tool wrapper seams in `test/tools/{git-tools,search-tools}.test.ts` (MEDIUM)
- Still use injected execution seams for deterministic error modeling.
- Mitigation: preserve for boundary failures; keep real-integration parity in git/search integration suites.

### Lower residuals
4. Artifact/template IO seam tests (LOW-MEDIUM)
- Acceptable for parser/formatter unit granularity; can be expanded opportunistically in future hardening.

## 5) Threshold recommendation (raise vs hold)
Given current evidence and pending C2 implementation:

- `src/git/*`, `src/search/*`: **RAISE** realism expectations next gate.
  - Rationale: substantial real-subprocess integration depth now exists.
  - Suggested C2 target: enforce higher module floors than global baseline.

- `src/scanner/*`, `src/index-manager/*`: **RAISE (moderate)**.
  - Rationale: real-FS edge coverage expanded; keep room for additional permission-denial hard cases.

- `src/discovery/*`, `src/tools/*` protocol path: **RAISE (contract-focused)**.
  - Rationale: transcript realism and malformed payload handling materially improved.

- `src/cli/*` + e2e runtime surfaces: **HOLD** until Track B (B2/B3/B4/B6) closes.
  - Rationale: realism migration still in progress; avoid premature hard floors.

- Artifact/template IO areas: **HOLD**.
  - Rationale: lower risk; not the current bottleneck for operational confidence.

## 6) Gate decision
- Track A readiness: **PASS (with residual risk explicitly documented)**.
- Recommendation: proceed to C2 threshold implementation using the raise/hold map above, while Track B continues to close CLI/e2e realism gaps.

title: Git Integration
description: Safe git command execution, diff modes, status collection, and patch token budgeting. All git commands are run with safety flags: --no-ext-diff, --no-textconv, --color=never, GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0. Supports diff modes: off, uncommitted, staged, unstaged, compare:<revspec>, main, <branch>, back:<N>. In review mode, changed files become high-priority hints for discovery, and diffs are included in the prompt with a strict token cap (--git-max-patch-tokens, default 6000). The git layer must handle edge cases gracefully: shallow clones, detached HEAD, missing remote, large diffs that exceed the token cap.

title: Safe git command runner
description:
A wrapper for spawning git commands with safety flags enforced. Every git invocation goes through this runner, which: (1) sets environment: GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0 (prevent interactive prompts that would hang), (2) adds flags: --no-ext-diff (don't use external diff tools), --no-textconv (don't use textconv filters), --color=never (machine-readable output), (3) enforces a timeout (default 30s — git should never take that long), (4) captures stdout, stderr, and exit code, (5) handles common failures: not a git repo (exit 128), no commits yet, permission denied.

The runner is used by: git status, git diff, git log, and any other git operations. It ensures consistent, safe behavior and prevents git from doing anything interactive or unpredictable. Returns a typed result: {ok: boolean, stdout: string, stderr: string, exitCode: number}.

title: Diff mode parsing and execution
description:
Parse and execute the --diff flag values:

- 'off': no diff (default)
- 'uncommitted': git diff HEAD (all uncommitted changes)
- 'staged': git diff --cached (staged changes only)
- 'unstaged': git diff (unstaged changes only)
- 'compare:<revspec>': git diff <revspec> (e.g., compare:main, compare:HEAD~3)
- 'main': git diff main...HEAD (changes since diverging from main)
- '<branch>': git diff <branch>...HEAD (changes since diverging from branch)
- 'back:<N>': git diff HEAD~N (last N commits)

Each mode maps to a specific git diff command. The diff output is parsed into a structured format: [{path, status (added/modified/deleted/renamed), hunks: [{oldStart, oldCount, newStart, newCount, content}]}]. The parser must handle all git diff output formats including renames, binary diffs (skip content, note as binary), and mode changes. Max files from a single diff: --git-max-files (default 20) — if more files changed, sort by hunk size and take the top N.

title: Git status collection
description: Collect git status for inclusion in the prompt's repo overview. Run 'git status --porcelain=v2 --branch' for machine-readable output. Parse into: {branch: string, upstream?: string, ahead: number, behind: number, changes: [{path, status: 'modified'|'added'|'deleted'|'renamed'|'untracked'}]}. Controlled by --git-status flag (default on). The git status is included in the repo overview section of the prompt to help the reasoning model understand what the user is currently working on. Keep the output compact: list changed files but don't include diffs here (those go in the git_diff section). Handle edge cases: no commits yet (empty status), detached HEAD (note in branch field), not a git repo (skip silently).

title: Patch token capping
description: When including git diffs in the prompt, enforce --git-max-patch-tokens (default 6000). Token estimation uses the same tokenEstimate() utility from core. If total patch tokens exceed the cap: (1) sort files by relevance (files that match task terms or are in the selection go first), (2) include files in order until budget exhausted, (3) for the last file that partially fits, truncate at a hunk boundary (don't cut in the middle of a hunk), (4) append a marker: '... ‹TRUNCATED: N more files, ~X tokens›'. This capping is separate from the main prompt budget — diff tokens come from their own pool. The cap prevents a large refactor's diff from consuming the entire prompt budget while still giving the reasoning model useful context about recent changes.

title: Search Engine
description: Content and path search with ripgrep integration and pure-JS fallback. The search engine is used by: (1) the tool host for agent file_search() calls, (2) offline discovery for task term matching, (3) the indexer for populating search data. Primary backend is ripgrep (spawned as subprocess), with a pure-JS fallback for environments without rg. All results must be deterministically formatted: sorted by descending hit count then ascending path, excerpts capped, truncation metadata included. This determinism is critical — slightly different search results cascade into different selections, breaking reproducibility.

title: Ripgrep subprocess integration
description:
Integrate ripgrep (rg) as the primary search backend via subprocess spawning. Functions needed:

- searchContent(pattern, options): search file contents, return hits with paths + line numbers + excerpts
- searchPaths(pattern, options): search file paths/names
- isRipgrepAvailable(): check if 'rg' is in PATH

Options: regex (bool), extensions filter, path filter, exclude patterns, max_results, context_lines.

Spawn with flags: --json (machine-readable output), --no-heading, --line-number, --color never, --max-count (cap results per file), --max-filesize (skip large files). Parse rg JSON output into our SearchResult type. Handle: rg not found (return availability=false for fallback), rg crashes (return error), no results (return empty array). Ripgrep is fast enough that we can use it for both agent tool calls and offline discovery without performance concerns. Timeout: 10s per search to prevent hangs on pathological patterns.

title: Fallback search implementation
description: Pure-JavaScript search fallback for environments without ripgrep. Used when isRipgrepAvailable() returns false. Implementation: read file contents from the scanner's file list, apply the search pattern (regex or literal), collect hits. This is obviously slower than ripgrep but ensures ctx works everywhere. Optimizations: (1) read files lazily (stream/readline), (2) stop searching a file after max_count hits, (3) respect the same max_results cap as ripgrep. The output format must be identical to the ripgrep backend — downstream code should not know or care which backend was used. The fallback also searches file paths (simple glob/regex matching against the path list from the scanner).

title: Deterministic search result formatting
description:
Format search results deterministically regardless of backend (ripgrep or fallback). Rules from the spec:

1. Results sorted by: (a) descending hit count, (b) ascending path (for ties)
2. Excerpts per file: choose earliest line numbers among top hits
3. Truncate excerpts to max_excerpt_chars (default 200), replacing overflow with '…'
4. Hard cap max_files (default 50 unless specified by tool call)
5. Max excerpts per file: 3 (default)
6. Always include truncation metadata object: {max_files, max_excerpts_per_file, max_excerpt_chars}

This formatting layer sits between the raw search backend and the tool result output. It ensures that the same content always produces the same search result structure, which is critical for reproducible discovery. The agent sees consistent, predictable results regardless of filesystem ordering or search backend implementation details.

title: Tool Protocol & Host
description: The tool host is the mediator between discovery agents and ctx internals. It parses agent tool calls (ctx_tool fenced blocks), dispatches to internal implementations, formats results (ctx_result blocks), validates the final ctx_final schema, and enforces all determinism/truncation/redaction rules regardless of agent behavior. The tool protocol is backend-agnostic — it works the same whether the agent is an OpenAI API model, a local Claude CLI, or the offline deterministic engine. Key invariant: even if the agent misbehaves (requests forbidden files, exceeds limits, produces invalid output), the tool host enforces all rules. Agent is advisory; ctx is authoritative.

title: Tool call envelope parser (ctx_tool blocks)
description:
Parse agent output to extract tool calls from ctx_tool fenced code blocks. The agent emits tool calls in this format:

```ctx_tool
{"id":"t1","tool":"file_search","args":{"pattern":"OAuth"}}
```

Parser requirements:
1. Extract all ctx_tool blocks from a chunk of agent text (may contain multiple)
2. Parse JSON within each block (validate: must have 'id' and 'tool' fields, 'args' optional)
3. Return ToolCall[]: {id: string, tool: string, args: Record<string, any>}
4. Handle malformed blocks: invalid JSON → return parse error for that block, continue with others
5. Handle missing required fields: return validation error
6. Blocks are executed sequentially in the order they appear (spec requirement)

The parser must be lenient about surrounding text — agents may emit explanations between tool calls. Only the fenced blocks matter. This parser is used by both API-based and CLI-based discovery backends.

title: Tool result envelope formatter (ctx_result blocks)
description:
Format tool execution results into ctx_result fenced code blocks for the agent. Success format:

```ctx_result
{"id":"t1","ok":true,"result":{...},"meta":{"truncated":false,"tokens_estimate":123}}
```

Error format:
```ctx_result
{"id":"t1","ok":false,"error":{"code":"READ_DENIED","message":"..."}}
```

The formatter: (1) serializes result/error to JSON, (2) includes meta.truncated (bool) and meta.tokens_estimate for successful results, (3) uses standard error codes: READ_DENIED, NOT_FOUND, INVALID_ARGS, BINARY_FILE, SIZE_EXCEEDED, SEARCH_FAILED, PARSE_FAILED, BUDGET_EXCEEDED, INTERNAL_ERROR. The result is always valid JSON wrapped in a ctx_result fence. The formatter also applies redaction to the result content before serializing (secrets in file content, search excerpts, etc.).

title: ctx_final schema validation
Tool execution dispatcher
description: Central dispatcher that routes parsed ToolCall objects to their implementations. The dispatcher: (1) receives a ToolCall, (2) validates the tool name against the known catalog (repo_info, file_tree, list_files, file_search, read_file, read_snippet, codemap, git_status, git_diff, select_add, select_remove, select_get, select_clear, token_estimate, budget_report), (3) validates args against the tool's parameter schema, (4) calls the implementation function, (5) applies redaction to the result, (6) applies truncation rules, (7) formats as ToolResult via the envelope formatter. Unknown tool names return INVALID_TOOL error. Invalid args return INVALID_ARGS with a message explaining what's wrong. The dispatcher also tracks execution stats (call count, total time, tokens returned) for the run record.

title: Deterministic truncation enforcer
description:
Enforce deterministic truncation rules on all tool results to prevent output size from varying between runs. Rules:

file_search results: max_files (50), max_excerpts_per_file (3), max_excerpt_chars (200)
codemap results: max_symbols (200 per file), max_signature_chars (160)
read_file results: always line-numbered (0001| ...), truncated at limit with '... ‹TRUNCATED: limit=N›' footer
file_tree results: max depth, max entries per level

The truncation enforcer sits between the tool implementation and the result formatter. It takes the raw result, applies caps, sorts deterministically, and returns the truncated result with a 'truncation' metadata object that describes what limits were applied. This metadata helps the agent understand when it's seeing a partial view and should refine its query. All truncation rules use stable ordering (defined in each tool's spec) so the same content always produces the same truncated output.

title: Discovery Runner
description: The discovery runner orchestrates codebase exploration to identify which files/symbols are relevant to the user's task. Three backends: (1) Offline deterministic — always available, uses task term extraction + index-based ranking + entrypoint heuristics + git-diff bias. (2) LLM via provider API — uses OpenAI/Anthropic/Google APIs with the tool protocol. (3) LLM via local CLI — adapters for claude-cli, codex-cli, gemini-cli. Auto-selection logic: airgap→offline, else API if configured→LLM, else CLI if available→local-cli, else→offline. Discovery agent is a RESEARCH agent only — it identifies context, not solutions. ctx enforces this by validating the ctx_final schema and ignoring any solution text the agent produces.

title: Task term extraction
description:
Extract searchable terms from the user's task text for offline discovery and search boosting. Strategy:

1. Split task into tokens (word boundaries, camelCase splitting, snake_case splitting)
2. Identify code identifiers: words matching [A-Z][a-z]+[A-Z] (camelCase), words with underscores, words with dots (module.path)
3. Identify file paths: tokens containing / or . with code extensions
4. Identify config keys: tokens matching common config patterns (ENV_VAR_STYLE, dotted.config.key)
5. Identify API endpoints: tokens starting with / that look like URL paths
6. Filter out stop words and common English words that would match too broadly
7. Deduplicate and normalize (lowercase for content search, preserve case for path search)

Output: {identifiers: string[], paths: string[], configKeys: string[], endpoints: string[], searchTerms: string[]}. These terms drive both offline discovery (which files to rank) and LLM discovery (seeding the agent's initial search). Quality of term extraction directly impacts offline discovery quality.

title: Turn management and timeout enforcement
description:
Manage the discovery agent conversation loop with turn limits and timeouts. Configuration:

- --agent-max-turns (default 20): maximum number of agent turns (tool call → result cycles)
- --agent-timeout (default 600s = 10 min): wall-clock timeout for entire discovery
- Per-API-call timeout: 60s (configurable)

Implementation:
1. Track turn count (increment after each tool execution cycle)
2. Track elapsed wall-clock time from discovery start
3. Before each API call, check both limits
4. If turn limit reached: tell agent 'This is your LAST turn. Please output your ctx_final block now.' Give it one more turn.
5. If timeout approaching: same last-turn message
6. If agent still doesn't produce ctx_final: extract partial selection via select_get(), construct synthetic DiscoveryResult, log warning
7. Track timing per turn for performance analysis

The turn limit prevents runaway agents from making 100 API calls. The timeout prevents slow APIs from blocking forever. Both are configurable because some tasks genuinely need more exploration.

title: LLM discovery fallback logic
description:
Handle all failure modes during LLM discovery by falling back gracefully:

Failure modes and responses:
1. No API key → fall back to offline discovery, warn on stderr
2. API auth error (401/403) → fall back to offline, warn 'invalid API key'
3. API rate limit (429) → retry with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s), max 3 retries, then fall back
4. API server error (5xx) → retry once, then fall back
5. Network error → fall back to offline, warn 'network unreachable'
6. Agent timeout → extract partial selection if any tools were called, merge with offline discovery
7. Agent produces invalid ctx_final → request retry if turns remain, else fall back
8. Agent never produces ctx_final → extract select_get() state, merge with offline

MERGE STRATEGY for partial LLM + offline: use the LLM's partial selection as high-priority seeds, then fill remaining budget with offline-ranked files. This hybrid approach salvages whatever useful work the agent did before failing.

All fallbacks STILL produce a prompt — ctx should almost never fail to output something useful. The only total failure mode is if the repo has zero readable files (exit code 3).

title: Claude CLI discovery adapter
description:
Adapter for running LLM discovery via the Claude CLI (claude command). Strategy:

1. Check availability: 'which claude' or 'claude --version'
2. Prepare a prompt file containing the discovery system prompt + task + repo overview
3. Invoke: claude --print --dangerously-skip-permissions -p '<discovery_prompt>' (non-interactive mode)
4. Capture stdout, parse for ctx_tool blocks
5. For each tool call: execute locally, format result, include in next invocation
6. Since Claude CLI is a one-shot tool, each 'turn' requires a new invocation with the full conversation history
7. Parse final output for ctx_final block

Limitations: Claude CLI may not support multi-turn conversations natively. The adapter may need to simulate turns by including the full conversation in each prompt. This is less efficient than API-based discovery but allows using Claude without an API key (uses the user's Claude subscription). This adapter is BEST-EFFORT — some Claude CLI versions may not work well for this use case.

title: Codex CLI discovery adapter
description:
Adapter for running LLM discovery via OpenAI's Codex CLI (codex command). Similar pattern to Claude CLI adapter:

1. Check availability: 'which codex' or 'codex --version'
2. Prepare discovery prompt
3. Invoke codex with the prompt (check codex CLI flags for non-interactive/batch mode)
4. Parse output for ctx_tool/ctx_final blocks
5. Handle turns (may need conversation simulation)

This adapter exists because some enterprises mandate using specific CLI tools rather than direct API access. The output quality depends on the underlying model (likely GPT-4-turbo or similar). Same fallback behavior as other adapters: if the CLI is unavailable or produces unparseable output, fall back to offline discovery.

title: Gemini CLI discovery adapter
description: Adapter for running LLM discovery via Google's Gemini CLI (gemini command). Same pattern as other CLI adapters. Lower priority since the Gemini CLI is newer and less widely adopted. Check availability, prepare prompt, invoke, parse output. The adapter should handle the Gemini CLI's specific output format and flags. Fall back to offline if unavailable or broken.

title: Backend auto-selection (--discover auto)
description:
Implement the --discover auto logic that selects the best available discovery backend:

1. If --privacy airgap → offline (non-negotiable)
2. If --no-llm → offline
3. Else if a provider API key is configured and the provider is reachable (quick health check) → LLM (API)
4. Else if a supported local CLI agent is available (check in order: config.local_cli.agent_priority) → local-cli
5. Else → offline

The health check for provider APIs should be lightweight: just verify the key format is valid and optionally make a minimal API call (list models). Don't spend >2s on health checks. For CLI agents: just check 'which <command>' — don't actually invoke them during selection.

Log the selected backend to stderr for transparency: 'Discovery: using openai API (gpt-4o)' or 'Discovery: using offline (no API key configured)'. This helps users understand why they're getting offline results when they expected LLM discovery.

title: Index-based file ranking for offline discovery
description:
Rank files by relevance to the task using index data. Scoring (all deterministic):

1. For each extracted task term, search the index for files containing that term (content match)
2. For each file path term, find files with matching path components
3. Score each file: sum of (term_frequency * term_weight) across all matching terms
4. Boost files that match multiple distinct terms (breadth bonus)
5. Boost files that are imported by other high-scoring files (import graph proximity)
6. Boost entrypoint files (see entrypoint heuristics task)
7. Normalize scores to 0-1000 range

Output: ranked file list with scores, sorted descending. This ranking is the PRIMARY discovery mechanism when no LLM is available. It must produce a useful selection on its own — the user who runs ctx --no-llm should still get a good prompt. The ranking algorithm is inspired by TF-IDF but simplified for speed and determinism.

title: Entrypoint heuristic detection
description:
Detect likely entrypoint files that serve as starting points for understanding the codebase. Heuristics:

1. Package entrypoints: main field in package.json, bin field entries, exports field
2. Framework entrypoints: app.ts/js, server.ts/js, main.ts/js, index.ts/js at src root
3. Route/controller files: files in routes/, controllers/, handlers/, api/ directories
4. CLI entrypoints: files with shebang lines (#!/usr/bin/env), bin/ directory files
5. Test entrypoints: test config files (jest.config, vitest.config, etc.)
6. Config entrypoints: .env.example, config/ directory files, settings files

Each heuristic has a confidence score. Files matching multiple heuristics get higher entrypoint scores. The entrypoint list is used by: (1) offline discovery to seed the selection, (2) priority scoring to boost entrypoint files, (3) repo_info() to include in build_hints. The heuristics should be language-aware where possible (Python: setup.py, __main__.py; Go: main.go in cmd/; Rust: main.rs, lib.rs).

title: Git-diff bias for discovery
description:
When git diff is enabled, use changed files to bias discovery toward recent work. This is especially important in review mode but also valuable in plan mode (recently changed files are likely relevant to the next task).

Implementation:
1. Get the list of changed files from the git layer
2. Boost their priority scores (see priority scoring algorithm)
3. Also boost files that IMPORT changed files (1-hop dependency)
4. Also boost test files that match changed modules (e.g., changed src/auth/login.ts → boost test/auth/login.test.ts)
5. In review mode: changed files are automatically added to the selection (full mode for small files, slices for large ones)

The bias is applied during both offline and LLM discovery. For offline, it directly affects ranking. For LLM, it's included in the agent's initial context (the agent sees which files changed and can prioritize accordingly). This makes ctx review mode particularly powerful — it automatically identifies the right context for a code review.

title: Offline deterministic discovery assembler
description:
The complete offline discovery pipeline that runs without any LLM. Steps:

1. Extract task terms
2. Run index-based file ranking
3. Apply entrypoint heuristics
4. Apply git-diff bias (if diff enabled)
5. Select top-N files based on combined scores:
   - Top ~5-8 files as 'full' (highest relevance, under size limit)
   - Next ~10-15 files as 'slices' (medium relevance)
   - Next ~20-30 files as 'codemap_only' (reference context)
6. Construct slices for 'slices' files (AST-aware, around task term matches)
7. Generate a synthetic handoff summary from the scoring data:
   - entrypoints: top-scored entrypoint files
   - key_modules: top-scored non-entrypoint files
   - data_flows: inferred from import graph between selected files
   - open_questions: none (offline can't ask questions)
8. Package as DiscoveryResult

This pipeline must be fast (target: <2s for 10k file repo) and produce a useful selection. It's the ALWAYS-AVAILABLE baseline — even if every LLM API is down, ctx still works.

title: Discovery agent system prompt
description:
Craft the system prompt given to LLM discovery agents. The prompt must clearly establish the agent's role and constraints:

ROLE: You are a codebase research agent. Your job is to explore this repository and identify the files, symbols, and context relevant to the user's task.

MUST DO: (1) Use the provided tools to search, read, and analyze code. (2) Propose a selection of files with appropriate modes (full/slices/codemap_only). (3) Produce a factual handoff summary with entrypoints, key modules, data flows, config knobs, and relevant tests. (4) Note any open questions with default assumptions. (5) Stay within the budget (provided as context).

MUST NOT: (1) Propose implementation approaches or solutions. (2) Write code. (3) Output step-by-step plans. (4) Speculate about how to fix bugs or implement features.

FORMAT: Use ctx_tool blocks for tool calls. End with a ctx_final block containing your selection and handoff summary.

The prompt also includes: task text, budget info, repo overview (from repo_info), and initial search hints (from task term extraction). This prompt is critical for discovery quality — it must be precise enough to prevent the agent from solving the task (that's the reasoning model's job) while still getting excellent context selection.

title: Provider API discovery adapter: OpenAI
description:
Adapter for running LLM discovery via the OpenAI API (gpt-4o, gpt-4-turbo, etc.). Implementation:

1. Initialize with API key (from env: OPENAI_API_KEY) and model name (from config or --model flag)
2. Send the discovery system prompt + initial user message (task + repo overview) to the chat completions API
3. Parse agent response for ctx_tool blocks
4. Execute tool calls via the tool dispatcher
5. Send tool results back as the next user message
6. Repeat until: agent emits ctx_final, max_turns reached, or timeout
7. If ctx_final received: validate and return DiscoveryResult
8. If timeout/max_turns: extract whatever partial selection the agent built via select_get(), generate a best-effort result

Use the OpenAI SDK or direct fetch. Handle API errors: rate limits (429 → retry with backoff, max 3 retries), auth errors (401 → fall back to offline), server errors (5xx → retry once, then fall back). Track token usage from API response for cost logging. The adapter must work with any model that supports chat completions (not just GPT-4 — also o1, o3, etc.).

title: Provider API discovery adapter: Anthropic
description:
Adapter for running LLM discovery via the Anthropic API (Claude). Implementation parallels the OpenAI adapter but uses Anthropic's messages API:

1. Initialize with API key (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) and model (claude-sonnet-4-20250514 default or --model)
2. Send system prompt as system parameter, task as first user message
3. Parse assistant response for ctx_tool blocks
4. Execute tools, send results back
5. Repeat until ctx_final or limits reached

Key differences from OpenAI: (1) system prompt is a separate parameter, not a system message, (2) response format may differ (handle both text and content blocks), (3) rate limits may have different headers. Use the Anthropic SDK (@anthropic-ai/sdk). Same fallback and error handling as OpenAI adapter. Claude models tend to be good at following the 'research only, no solutions' instruction, but the system prompt should still be explicit about it.

title: Provider API discovery adapter: Google
description:
Adapter for running LLM discovery via the Google Gemini API. Implementation:

1. Initialize with API key (GOOGLE_API_KEY) and model (gemini-pro or --model)
2. Use the Gemini API's generateContent endpoint
3. Send system instruction + task content
4. Parse response for ctx_tool blocks
5. Execute tools, send results back as next turn
6. Repeat until ctx_final or limits

Differences from OpenAI/Anthropic: (1) different API structure (contents array with parts), (2) different system instruction format, (3) different rate limit behavior. Use the Google AI SDK (@google/generative-ai). Same error handling and fallback pattern. This adapter is lower priority than OpenAI and Anthropic but important for users who prefer Google's models. The tool protocol (ctx_tool/ctx_result/ctx_final) is the same regardless of provider — that's the whole point of having a backend-agnostic protocol.

title: Modularize performance benchmark runner and add helper tests
close_reason: Completed: moved benchmark engine into src/perf with reusable target evaluation and kept test/performance entrypoint as wrapper; added benchmark helper tests and validated benchmark:ci path

title: Privacy & Redaction
description: Secret detection, redaction engine, never-include enforcement, and privacy mode routing. This cross-cutting concern applies at two points: (1) tool outputs returned to the discovery agent, and (2) the final assembled prompt. Redaction replaces sensitive substrings with '‹REDACTED:reason›' while preserving line structure (critical for keeping line numbers stable in slices). Three privacy modes: 'normal' (full LLM access subject to redaction), 'strict' (LLM discovery allowed but agent sees only codemaps + minimal snippets), 'airgap' (no external calls, offline only). The redaction library covers: common secret patterns (AWS keys, GitHub tokens, JWTs, private keys), user-provided regex, and optionally entropy-based high-risk string detection (with care to avoid false positives).

title: Secret pattern detection library
description:
Build a library of regex patterns for detecting secrets in source code. Categories:

1. API keys: AWS (AKIA...), GitHub (ghp_, gho_, ghs_, ghr_), Stripe (sk_live_, sk_test_), Google (AIza...), Slack (xoxb-, xoxp-, xoxs-)
2. Tokens: JWT (eyJ...), Bearer tokens, OAuth tokens
3. Private keys: RSA/EC/DSA private key headers (-----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY-----)
4. Connection strings: postgres://, mysql://, mongodb://, redis://  with credentials
5. Generic high-entropy strings: optional entropy-based detection (Shannon entropy > threshold on base64/hex strings > 20 chars)

Each pattern has: regex, category name, severity (high/medium), false_positive_likelihood. Store as a typed array of PatternEntry objects. The entropy-based detector should be conservative — emit markers rather than redact (false positives on hashes, encoded data, etc. are annoying). Users can add custom patterns via config.privacy.extra_redact_patterns.

title: Redaction engine
description:
Core redaction function that processes text and replaces detected secrets with markers. Input: text string + config (patterns, custom patterns, mode). Output: redacted text with all secret substrings replaced by '‹REDACTED:category›' (e.g., '‹REDACTED:aws_key›', '‹REDACTED:jwt›').

Key requirements:
1. Preserve line structure — redaction must NOT add or remove lines. This is critical for keeping line numbers stable in slices.
2. Preserve surrounding context — only replace the sensitive substring, not the whole line.
3. Apply all patterns in a single pass (avoid redacting already-redacted markers).
4. Track redaction count and categories for the manifest/report.
5. Support disabling redaction (--redact off) for cases where the user knows the content is safe.

The engine is applied at two points: (a) tool results returned to the discovery agent, (b) the final prompt output. Both use the same engine but may have different configs (e.g., final prompt redaction might be disabled if the user trusts their local model).

title: Never-include path enforcement
description:
Implement the --never-include glob patterns as an unconditional exclusion layer that CANNOT be overridden by the discovery agent, --include flags, or any other mechanism. Default never-include patterns: **/.env, **/*secret*, **/*private_key*. User can add more via config.privacy.never_include or --never-include CLI flag.

Enforcement points:
1. File walker: never-include files are filtered out during scanning (never appear in FileEntry[])
2. Tool host: if agent requests read_file() on a never-include path, return READ_DENIED error
3. Selection manager: never-include files cannot be added to selection (silently ignored)

This is the strongest privacy guarantee — even a misbehaving agent cannot access these files. The manifest should log which files were excluded by never-include (paths only, not content) for transparency.

title: Privacy mode routing
description:
Implement the three privacy modes and their behavioral constraints:

NORMAL: LLM discovery allowed. Full file reads via tools. Redaction applied. This is the default — maximum discovery quality with secret protection.

STRICT: LLM discovery allowed but restricted. Agent can only see codemaps + minimal snippets (read_file returns max 20 lines centered on a match, not the full file). read_snippet still works but with a tight line limit. The final prompt may still include full/slices for the reasoning model. This mode explicitly trades discovery quality for privacy — the user accepts this tradeoff.

AIRGAP: No external network calls whatsoever. Forces --discover offline. Provider API keys are ignored even if present. No telemetry, no update checks, nothing leaves the machine. Strictly offline deterministic discovery only.

The privacy mode is checked by: tool host (restricts reads in strict mode), discovery runner (forces offline in airgap), and the config merger (validates consistency — e.g., --discover llm + --privacy airgap is an error).

title: Normalize dynamic run_id fields in golden snapshot harness

comments.author: Neil Pattanaik
comments.text: Completed by adding normalization in test/cli/golden-snapshots.test.ts for dynamic run IDs in stdout/stderr and loaded snapshots. This keeps golden assertions stable while preserving substantive prompt snapshot checks after canonical run_id propagation. Validation: bun test test/cli/golden-snapshots.test.ts; bun test (full suite).

title: Artifacts & Explainability
description: Run records, manifest output, explain output, and the ctx open command. Every run produces artifacts stored in .ctx/runs/<run_id>/: input task, normalized terms, discovery backend used, tool call log (optional), selection manifest (always), final prompt (optional), timing and token reports. 'ctx explain last' shows why each file was included/excluded and what degradation was applied. 'ctx manifest last' outputs machine-readable JSON. 'ctx open last' opens the prompt in $PAGER. Run IDs are deterministic: timestamp + short hash. This layer is essential for debuggability — when the prompt doesn't include what the user expected, explain shows exactly why.

title: Run ID generation and run record storage
description:
Generate deterministic run IDs and persist run records.

Run ID format: ISO timestamp + short repo hash. Example: '20240115T143022-a1b2c3'. Generated at pipeline start, used to name the artifacts directory.

Run record structure: {runId, task (verbatim), normalizedTerms, config (serialized merged config), discoveryBackend, discoveryDuration, toolCallLog (if --verbose), selection (final, post-budget), tokenReport, timing: {scan, index, discovery, budget, assembly, total}, startedAt, completedAt}

Storage: .ctx/runs/<run_id>/run.json (the complete record), .ctx/runs/<run_id>/prompt.md (the generated prompt, if config.output.store_runs is true). Controlled by config.output.store_runs (default true) and config.output.runs_dir (default '.ctx/runs'). Also maintain a .ctx/runs/latest symlink pointing to the most recent run (for 'ctx explain last').

Run records enable reproducibility analysis, performance tracking, and debugging. They're the audit trail.

title: ctx explain command
description:
Implement 'ctx explain (last|<run-id>)'. Reads the run record and outputs a human-readable explanation:

1. TASK: what was the task
2. DISCOVERY: which backend was used, how many turns, how long
3. SELECTION: for each selected file:
   - Path
   - Mode (full/slices/codemap_only) and WHY (agent selected it, or offline ranked it high, or it was force-included)
   - Priority score breakdown (which scoring components contributed)
   - If degraded: what it was degraded FROM and WHY (budget constraint)
4. DROPPED: files that were considered but dropped, with reasons (low priority, budget, size limit, binary, never-include)
5. TOKEN BUDGET: budget, initial estimate, final estimate, per-section breakdown, per-file breakdown (top 10 largest)
6. DEGRADATIONS: list of degradation actions in order they were applied

Format as a clear, readable report. This is the primary debugging tool when the prompt doesn't include expected context — the user can see exactly what happened and why.

title: ctx manifest command
description:
Implement 'ctx manifest (last|<run-id>) [-o <path>]'. Outputs machine-readable JSON:

{
  repo: {root, name, totalFiles, languages},
  config: {budget, mode, format, privacy, discover, diff},
  discovery: {backend, model?, turns?, duration},
  selection: [{path, mode, priority, priorityScore, rationale, slices?: [{startLine, endLine, description}], tokenEstimate}],
  dropped: [{path, reason, priorityScore?}],
  tokenReport: {budget, estimated, bySection: {files, codemaps, tree, metadata, diff}, byFile: [{path, tokens}]},
  degradations: [{step, action, path, fromMode, toMode, tokensSaved}],
  git: {diff_mode, changed_files_count, patch_tokens}
}

If -o <path> is provided, write to file; otherwise stdout. This JSON is designed for tooling integration — CI systems, dashboards, custom analysis scripts. The schema should be stable across versions (add fields, don't remove).

title: ctx open command
description:
Implement 'ctx open (last|<run-id>)'. Opens the generated prompt file in the user's $PAGER (or 'less' as default). Steps:

1. Resolve run ID ('last' → latest symlink)
2. Check that .ctx/runs/<id>/prompt.md exists (error if store_runs was off)
3. Spawn $PAGER with the file path
4. Wait for pager to exit

This is a convenience command for reviewing what ctx generated. Simple but useful. If the prompt file doesn't exist (runs storage disabled), suggest: 'Enable config.output.store_runs to save prompts for later viewing.'

title: Agent-Facing Tool Implementations
description: Implementations of all tools available to discovery agents via the tool protocol: repo_info, file_tree, list_files, file_search, read_file, read_snippet, codemap, git_status, git_diff, select_add/remove/get/clear, token_estimate, budget_report. Each tool has strictly defined input/output schemas, deterministic result formatting, and hard truncation rules. These tools are the agent's ONLY interface to the codebase — the agent cannot access the filesystem directly. This mediation layer is what makes ctx safe and deterministic even with an unpredictable LLM agent.

title: repo_info() tool implementation
description: Implement the repo_info() agent-facing tool. Returns a JSON object with: repo_root (display name), total_files (count from scanner), language_stats ({language: fileCount} sorted by count descending), index_status ('fresh'|'stale'|'none'), ignore_summary (number of gitignore patterns, number of config ignores), build_hints (detected package managers, build tools: package.json→npm/bun, Cargo.toml→rust, go.mod→go, requirements.txt→python, Makefile, etc.), and module_map (from the codemap engine's module map generator). This is typically the FIRST tool the agent calls to orient itself in the codebase. It should be fast (<100ms from cache) and compact (<2000 tokens). No arguments needed.
close_reason: Completed: implemented repo_info tool + tests + export wiring; full test/build green

title: file_tree() tool implementation
description:
Implement file_tree({mode, max_depth?, path?}). Modes:
- 'auto': full tree for small repos (<200 files), folders-only for larger repos
- 'full': complete file tree with all files
- 'folders': directories only, with file counts
- 'selected': only files in the current selection + their parent directories

Format as an indented text tree (like the 'tree' command). max_depth limits nesting (default: no limit for auto/full, 3 for folders). path limits to a subtree. Sort: directories first, then files, both alphabetical. Include file counts per directory in brackets: 'src/auth/ [5 files]'. Truncate if total lines exceed 500 with '... (N more entries)'. The tree output is used in the prompt's <file_tree> section to give the reasoning model spatial awareness of the codebase.

title: file_search() tool implementation
description:
Implement file_search({pattern, mode, regex?, filter?, context_lines?, max_results?}). Modes:
- 'content': search file contents (delegates to search engine)
- 'path': search file paths/names
- 'both': search content and paths, merge results
- 'auto': if pattern looks like a path (contains / or .), use 'both'; else use 'content'

filter: {extensions?: string[], paths?: string[], exclude?: string[]}
context_lines: number of lines around each match (default 0)
max_results: cap on total results (default 50)

The implementation delegates to the search engine, applies the filter, and formats results through the deterministic truncation enforcer. Results include: path, hit count, top_excerpts (line number + excerpt text). This is the agent's primary exploration tool — it's how the agent discovers which files are relevant to the task.

title: read_file() and read_snippet() tool implementations
description:
Two file reading tools:

read_file({path, start_line?, limit?}): Read file content. If start_line provided, start from that line. If limit provided, read only that many lines. Output is always line-numbered when config.line_numbers is on: '0001| import { Router } from ...'. If truncated by limit, append '... ‹TRUNCATED: limit=N›'. Check against never-include (return READ_DENIED), check binary (return BINARY_FILE error), check size limit. In strict privacy mode, limit to 20 lines max per read.

read_snippet({path, anchor, before, after}): Read a snippet centered on an anchor (line number or symbol name). If anchor is a number, read lines [anchor-before, anchor+after]. If anchor is a string, search for it in the file and center on first match. Same permission/binary/privacy checks as read_file. This tool is useful for focused reads when the agent knows approximately where to look.

title: codemap() agent tool implementation
description: Implement codemap({paths, detail?, max_symbols?, max_results?}). Returns code structure for specified paths. paths can be file paths or directory paths. If a directory is given, return aggregated codemaps for all files in that directory. detail: 'summary' (default) or 'complete'. Delegates to the codemap engine for symbol extraction, applies deterministic formatting: symbols sorted by line number, signatures truncated to max_signature_chars. Result format: {paths, detail, results: [{path, language, lines, symbols: [{kind, signature, line}], truncation}]}. This tool lets the agent understand code structure without reading full file contents — essential for navigating large codebases efficiently.

title: git_status() and git_diff() agent tool implementations
description:
Wrap git integration for agent access:

git_status(): Returns {branch, upstream?, ahead, behind, changes: [{path, status}]}. Delegates to the git layer's status collection. Simple pass-through with deterministic formatting.

git_diff({compare, detail?, scope?, max_files?}):
- compare: diff mode string (uncommitted, staged, etc.)
- detail: 'summary' (file list + stats) | 'patches' (include patch content) | 'full' (complete diff)
- scope: 'all' | 'selected' (only files in current selection)
- max_files: cap on files returned

Delegates to the git layer's diff engine. In 'patches'/'full' mode, applies patch token capping. Returns {compare, files: [{path, status, stats: {additions, deletions}, hunks?}]}. These tools help the agent understand what the user has been working on recently, which is valuable context for discovery.

title: Selection building tools (select_add/remove/get/clear)
description:
Agent-facing tools for proposing file selections:

select_add({path, mode, slices?, priority?, rationale}): Add a file to the selection. Validates path exists, checks against hard constraints, checks never-include. Returns success or error (with reason). The selection is advisory — ctx may override during budget normalization.

select_remove({path}): Remove a file from selection. Returns success or NOT_FOUND.

select_get({view}): Get current selection state. Views:
- 'summary': file count, total estimated tokens, mode breakdown
- 'files': list of selected files with modes and priorities
- 'content': preview of what would be included (token estimates per file)
- 'codemaps': codemaps for codemap_only files

select_clear(): Reset selection to empty.

These tools give the agent iterative control over the selection, but all decisions are subject to ctx's enforcement layer.

title: token_estimate() and budget_report() tool implementations
description:
Budget awareness tools for the agent:

token_estimate({text?, path?, selection?}): Estimate tokens for: (a) raw text string, (b) a file path's content, or (c) the entire current selection. Uses the core token estimation utility. Returns {tokens: number, method: 'char_ratio', ratio: 3.5}. Helps the agent stay within budget during discovery.

budget_report(): Returns a comprehensive budget summary: {budget: number, estimated_total: number, remaining: number, breakdown: {files_full: {count, tokens}, files_slices: {count, tokens}, codemaps: {count, tokens}, tree: {tokens}, metadata: {tokens}, diff: {tokens}}, constraints: {max_files: {limit, used}, max_full_files: {limit, used}}}. This helps the agent make informed decisions about what to include — should it add another full file or switch to codemap_only?
