title: 2pv: SQLite corruption and prefix-mismatch forensic lane

comments.author: Neil Pattanaik
comments.text:
Forensic findings (ctx-2pv child lane)

## Repro confirmations
- `ctx-1as.6` remains non-mutable on main DB:
  - `br update ctx-1as.6 --status in_progress --json` -> `DATABASE_ERROR: internal error: cursor must be on a leaf to delete`
  - `br dep remove ctx-1as.6 ctx-1as --json` -> same DB error
- Other issues mutate normally (e.g. `ctx-1az` status update succeeds), so failure is issue-specific/path-specific.

## DB integrity signals
- Direct Bun SQLite read of `.beads/beads.db` fails immediately with:
  - `SQLiteError: database disk image is malformed (SQLITE_CORRUPT)`
- `br doctor --json` is inconsistent:
  - reports `sqlite.integrity_check: ok`
  - but simultaneously reports `schema.tables` missing core tables (`issues`, `dependencies`, etc.)
  - while also reporting matching DB/JSONL counts.

## Prefix-state signals
- JSONL contains mixed prefixes (`bd-*` + `ctx-*`), currently 2 `bd-*` IDs.
- Commands with auto-import can fail on prefix validation (`expected ctx, found bd-*`).
- `br sync --import-only --rename-prefix` does not currently normalize those `bd-*` IDs in JSONL in this workspace.

## Recovery attempts tested
- `br sync --import-only --rename-prefix` on main DB: no change to mixed-prefix JSONL; does not fix `ctx-1as.6` mutation.
- `br sync --import-only --rename-prefix --rebuild` on main DB: completes but `ctx-1as.6` still non-mutable.
- Fresh alternate DB imports:
  - with `--rebuild` can fail with same cursor-leaf error during orphan removal,
  - without rebuild may auto-detect `bd` prefix and make `ctx-*` IDs unreachable in that DB.

## Working interim workaround
- For normal work (non-`ctx-1as.6`) commands can proceed with:
  - `--no-auto-import --allow-stale`
- This bypasses transient prefix-import guard, but does not solve the core `ctx-1as.6` mutation defect.

title: Selection & Budget Manager
description: Selection state management, priority scoring, hard constraint enforcement, slice construction, and the deterministic degradation ladder. This is the HEART of ctx's determinism guarantee. The selection manager tracks which files are selected and in what mode (full/slices/codemap_only). The budget manager estimates total prompt tokens and applies a fixed-order degradation sequence when overbudget: (1) degrade lowest-priority full→slices, (2) degrade slices→codemap_only, (3) drop lowest-priority codemap_only, (4) shrink codemap detail, (5) shrink slices, (6) reduce tree verbosity, (7) warn or fail. Priority scoring combines: explicit includes (highest), git-changed files, entrypoint heuristics, hit density, import proximity, and agent priority field (weak signal). Tie-breaking uses stable lexicographic path order. All of this MUST be deterministic — same inputs → same outputs, always.

title: Selection state management
description:
Implement the SelectionManager class that tracks which files are selected and in what mode. Internal state: a Map<path, SelectionEntry> where SelectionEntry includes: path, mode (full|slices|codemap_only), priority (core|support|ref), rationale, slices (if mode=slices), and a numeric priorityScore for sorting.

Operations:
- add(entry): add or update a selection entry (validates against hard constraints)
- remove(path): remove from selection
- get(path): get a single entry
- getAll(): get all entries sorted by priorityScore (deterministic)
- clear(): reset selection
- toSummary(): compact representation for agent budget_report
- toManifest(): full manifest for artifacts

The selection manager does NOT enforce budget — that's the budget manager's job. It enforces hard constraints: max_files, max_full_files, binary exclusion, never-include. If add() would violate a hard constraint, it returns an error (not an exception) that the caller handles.

title: Priority scoring algorithm
description:
Compute a deterministic numeric priority score for each file to determine inclusion order and degradation order. Score components (weighted, all deterministic):

1. EXPLICIT_INCLUDE (+1000): file matches --include or --entrypoint flag
2. TASK_MENTION (+500): file path appears in the task text
3. GIT_CHANGED (+400 in review mode, +100 otherwise): file appears in git diff/status
4. ENTRYPOINT_HEURISTIC (+300): file matches entrypoint patterns (main.*, index.*, app.*, routes.*, server.*, cli.*)
5. HIT_DENSITY (+0-200): normalized count of task term matches in this file (from search), scaled to 0-200 range
6. IMPORT_PROXIMITY (+0-150): how many hops from a core file (shortest path in import graph, inverse scored)
7. AGENT_PRIORITY (+50 for core, +25 for support, +0 for ref): agent's advisory priority (weakest signal)

Total score = sum of applicable components. Tie-breaking: stable lexicographic path sort. The score is computed once during selection finalization and stored in SelectionEntry.priorityScore. Files with higher scores are kept longer during budget degradation.

title: Hard constraint enforcement
description:
Enforce all hard limits regardless of discovery agent behavior:

1. max_files (default 80): total files in selection cannot exceed this
2. max_full_files (default 10): files in 'full' mode cannot exceed this
3. max_slices_per_file (default 4): slices per file in 'slices' mode
4. max_file_bytes (1.5MB): files exceeding this are excluded (already handled by scanner, but double-check here)
5. binary exclusion: binary files cannot be selected
6. never-include: paths matching never-include patterns cannot be selected

When a hard constraint is violated, the constraint enforcer applies deterministic resolution:
- max_full_files exceeded: degrade lowest-priority full→slices until within limit
- max_files exceeded: drop lowest-priority entries until within limit
- max_slices_per_file exceeded: merge closest slices until within limit

These constraints are ALWAYS enforced — they cannot be overridden by the agent, by config, or by CLI flags. They are the safety rails that keep ctx predictable and bounded.

title: AST-aware slice construction
description:
When a file needs to be sliced (either because the agent specified slices, or because budget degradation demoted it from full), construct meaningful slices that respect code structure.

Strategy:
1. If agent provided slice ranges: validate and use them (expand to enclosing function/class boundaries using tree-sitter if available)
2. If no ranges provided (degradation case): find task term matches in the file, then expand each match to its enclosing function/class boundary using tree-sitter AST
3. Fallback (no tree-sitter for this language): expand ±30 lines around each match
4. Merge overlapping slices (important: don't include the same code twice)
5. Cap at max_slices_per_file
6. Label each slice: path, Lx-Ly, description (auto-generated from enclosing symbol name), rationale

The goal: when we can't include the whole file, include the MOST RELEVANT PARTS with enough context to be useful. A 500-line file might produce 2-3 slices totaling 80 lines — the critical functions. This is much better than the first 80 lines (which might just be imports and boilerplate).

title: Deterministic budget degradation ladder
description:
The heart of ctx's budget guarantee. When estimated tokens exceed --budget, apply degradation in this FIXED ORDER:

Step 1: Degrade lowest-priority 'full' files → 'slices' (one at a time, re-estimate after each)
Step 2: Degrade lowest-priority 'slices' files → 'codemap_only' (one at a time)
Step 3: Drop lowest-priority 'codemap_only' files entirely (one at a time)
Step 4: Shrink codemap detail: complete → summary for remaining codemap entries
Step 5: Shrink slice windows: reduce context lines from ±30 → ±20 → ±10, merge closer overlaps
Step 6: Reduce tree verbosity: full → selected → none
Step 7: If STILL over budget: emit warning to stderr. If --fail-on-overbudget, exit with code 6.

Critical invariant: this ladder is deterministic. Same selection + same budget = same degradation decisions = same final prompt. The ladder processes files in ascending priority order (lowest priority degraded first). After each degradation step, re-estimate tokens. Stop as soon as we're within budget. Log each degradation decision for the explain report.

title: Budget estimation and normalization loop
description:
The main budget loop that ties selection, estimation, and degradation together:

1. Take the raw selection from discovery (agent or offline)
2. Estimate total tokens: sum of (file content tokens + codemap tokens + tree tokens + metadata/template tokens + diff tokens)
3. Compare against --budget (default 60000)
4. If within budget: done, output the selection
5. If over budget: run the degradation ladder (step by step, re-estimate after each change)
6. After degradation: final estimate. Log the budget report: {budget, initial_estimate, final_estimate, degradations: [{action, target_path, from_mode, to_mode, tokens_saved}]}

The token estimation for each section:
- files (full): tokenEstimate(fileContent)
- files (slices): tokenEstimate(slice contents + labels)
- codemaps: tokenEstimate(formatted codemap)
- tree: tokenEstimate(formatted tree)
- template/metadata: ~500 tokens fixed overhead
- diff: separate pool (--git-max-patch-tokens)

The normalization loop is the bridge between 'what the agent wants' and 'what fits in the budget'. It preserves the agent's intent (high-priority files stay, low-priority ones get degraded) while guaranteeing the output fits.

title: Swap index driver to better-sqlite3 when Bun/native toolchain support is available
CLI e2e coverage for task source precedence (positional/stdin/task-file)
Config Manager
description: Configuration loading, merging, and validation. Handles the 4-level precedence: CLI flags > repo .ctx/config.toml > user ~/.config/ctx/config.toml > built-in defaults. Also handles environment variable overrides (CTX_REPO, CTX_BUDGET, etc.). The config schema is TOML-based and covers all 7 config sections: defaults, repo, index, discovery, local_cli, git, privacy, output. This component is critical because every other component reads from the merged config — it must be stable and well-tested before anything else is built on top.

title: Config schema definition and defaults
description:
Define the complete config schema as TypeScript types AND as a defaults object. The schema mirrors the TOML structure with 8 sections:

[defaults]: mode, format, budget_tokens (60000), reserve_tokens (15000), tree_mode, codemaps, max_files (80), max_full_files (10), max_slices_per_file (4), line_numbers (true)
[repo]: root, use_gitignore (true), ignore patterns, max_file_bytes (1500000), skip_binary (true)
[index]: enabled (true), engine ('sqlite'|'memory'), rebuild_on_schema_change (true)
[discovery]: discover ('auto'), provider, model, timeout_seconds (600), max_turns (20)
[local_cli]: agent_priority, cli commands for each agent
[git]: diff ('off'), git_status (true), max_files (20), max_patch_tokens (6000)
[privacy]: mode ('normal'), redact (true), never_include patterns, extra_redact_patterns
[output]: include_manifest_footer (true), include_token_report (true), path_display ('relative'), store_runs (true), runs_dir ('.ctx/runs')

The defaults object must be complete — every field has a value. This is the fallback when no config file exists.
close_reason: Completed: added full typed config schema defaults and tests

title: TOML config file parser
description: Implement TOML parsing for .ctx/config.toml and ~/.config/ctx/config.toml. Use a TOML parsing library (e.g., @iarna/toml or smol-toml — prefer smol-toml for smaller bundle). The parser must: (1) read the file if it exists (silently skip if not), (2) parse TOML into a plain object, (3) validate against the schema (warn on unknown keys, error on invalid values), (4) return a partial config (only the fields present in the file). Validation should be strict on types but lenient on missing fields (they come from defaults). Handle edge cases: file exists but is empty, file has syntax errors (warn + skip), file has unknown sections (warn + ignore).
close_reason: Completed: TOML config parser with validation/warnings and tests

title: Config precedence merging
description: Implement the 4-level config merge: CLI flags > repo config > user config > defaults. The merge must be deep (section-level), not shallow. For example, if repo config sets [git].diff='uncommitted' but doesn't set [git].max_files, the default max_files=20 should still apply. CLI flags override individual fields within sections (e.g., --budget overrides [defaults].budget_tokens). The merge function takes: (defaults, userConfig?, repoConfig?, cliOverrides?) and returns a fully populated CtxConfig. Every field in the result must have a value — no undefined. This is the single source of truth that all components read from.
close_reason: Completed: deep config precedence merge with tests

title: Environment variable overrides
description: Support environment variables that override config: CTX_REPO → repo.root, CTX_BUDGET → defaults.budget_tokens, CTX_MODE → defaults.mode, CTX_FORMAT → defaults.format, CTX_DISCOVER → discovery.discover, CTX_PROVIDER → discovery.provider, CTX_MODEL → discovery.model. Also detect provider API keys from standard env vars: OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, GOOGLE_API_KEY (these are NOT stored in config but used by discovery adapters). Env vars slot into the precedence between CLI flags and repo config (CLI > env > repo > user > defaults). Parse numeric values (CTX_BUDGET) with validation. Invalid values should warn and fall through to next precedence level.
close_reason: Completed: env override parsing with validation and provider key detection

title: Prompt Assembler
description: Assembles the final paste-ready prompt from discovery results, selected context, and mode-specific templates. Supports 4 output formats: markdown, markdown+xmltags (default), xml, plain. Supports 4 built-in modes: plan (architecture planning), question (code explanation), review (code review), context (raw context only). Custom templates can be added in .ctx/templates/*.md with YAML frontmatter. Placeholders: {{TASK}}, {{OPEN_QUESTIONS}}, {{TREE}}, {{REPO_OVERVIEW}}, {{HANDOFF_SUMMARY}}, {{CODEMAPS}}, {{FILES}}, {{GIT_DIFF}}, {{TOKEN_REPORT}}, {{MANIFEST}}. The assembler's output goes to stdout — it must be clean, with zero debug noise. All progress/warnings go to stderr.

title: Template placeholder engine
Built-in prompt templates (plan/question/review/context)
description:
Embed the 4 default templates from the spec as string constants in the codebase:

1. PLAN template: Full planning instructions (outcome/acceptance criteria, assumptions, architecture, file-by-file plan, implementation sequence, test strategy, risks)
2. QUESTION template: Grounded explanation instructions (reference files/lines, trace call flows, enumerate behaviors)
3. REVIEW template: Code review instructions (summary, findings by severity, API changes, error handling, security, tests)
4. CONTEXT template: No instructions — raw curated context only

Each template includes the standard section structure: ctx_metadata, task, open_questions, repo_overview, file_tree, discovery_handoff_summary, codemaps, files, git_diff, instructions (mode-specific), token_report, manifest. The plan and review templates are the most detailed; context is the simplest. Templates are selected by --mode flag. See PLAN.md sections 20.1-20.4 for the complete template text.

title: Custom template loading from .ctx/templates/
description:
Support user-defined templates in .ctx/templates/*.md. Templates can have YAML frontmatter:

---
name: team_plan
description: 'Plan with ADR and rollout gates'
---

The template body uses the same {{PLACEHOLDER}} syntax as built-in templates. Loading:
1. Scan .ctx/templates/ for .md files
2. Parse frontmatter (extract name, description)
3. Register template by name (frontmatter name or filename without extension)
4. When --mode matches a custom template name, use it instead of the built-in

The 'ctx templates list' subcommand shows all available templates (built-in + custom). 'ctx templates show <name>' shows the template content. Custom templates can override built-in ones (e.g., a custom 'plan' template replaces the default plan). This allows teams to standardize their prompt format while using the same ctx discovery pipeline.
close_reason: Completed in-tree (custom template loading/frontmatter/list/get APIs + tests) and validated by full bun test/build

title: Output format variants (markdown, markdown+xmltags, xml, plain)
description:
Implement the 4 output formats:

1. markdown+xmltags (default): Markdown content wrapped in XML-like tags (<task>, <files>, etc.). The XML tags help models parse sections reliably. Content within tags is Markdown. This is the recommended format for most models.

2. markdown: Pure Markdown with ## headers for sections instead of XML tags. More readable for humans but slightly harder for models to parse reliably.

3. xml: Pure XML with content in CDATA sections where needed. Most structured format. Some models work better with pure XML.

4. plain: Minimal formatting. Section headers as simple labels, no XML tags, no Markdown headers. Smallest token overhead.

Implementation: the section renderers produce content, and the format variant wraps them appropriately. The format is selected by --format flag. The template placeholders are format-agnostic — the format layer wraps the final assembled template, not individual sections.

title: Section renderers for prompt assembly
description:
Individual renderers for each prompt section:

1. METADATA: key-value pairs (repo root, run ID, mode, budget, etc.)
2. TASK: the user's task text (verbatim)
3. OPEN_QUESTIONS: formatted list from discovery handoff (question + why + default assumption)
4. REPO_OVERVIEW: build hints, language stats, git status summary
5. TREE: file tree (from file_tree() with appropriate mode setting)
6. HANDOFF_SUMMARY: entrypoints, key modules, data flows, config knobs, tests — all from discovery
7. CODEMAPS: formatted codemaps for codemap_only files + selected module overviews
8. FILES: file contents (full files with line numbers, slices with labels + line ranges)
9. GIT_DIFF: formatted patches with file headers
10. TOKEN_REPORT: budget summary (budget, estimated, by-section breakdown)
11. MANIFEST: compact file list with modes, priorities, rationales

Each renderer takes structured data (from discovery, selection, git) and produces formatted text. Line numbering for FILES uses the format '0001| <line content>' when config.line_numbers is on. Slices include headers: '--- src/auth/login.ts L120-L220 (login route handler) ---'.
close_reason: Completed in-tree (section renderers + tests) and validated by full bun test/build

title: Codemap Engine
description: Tree-sitter-based code structure extraction, caching, and progressive disclosure. The codemap engine parses source files to extract symbols (functions, classes, interfaces, exports, types) with their signatures and line numbers. Key design: codemaps are computed/cached for many files but NOT dumped wholesale into agent context. Instead, repo_info() includes a compact 'module map' (top directories + primary languages + capped high-level symbols), and the agent requests deeper codemaps via codemap({paths:[...]}) as needed. This progressive disclosure is what makes ctx scalable — computing 10k codemaps is fine as long as we only serialize the ones the agent asks for. Cache is keyed by file content hash for invalidation.

title: Tree-sitter parser setup (multi-language)
description: Set up tree-sitter for parsing source files across multiple languages. Use tree-sitter bindings for bun/Node.js (e.g., web-tree-sitter or node-tree-sitter). Languages to support initially: TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, Java, C, C++, Ruby, Swift, Kotlin, C#. Each language needs its grammar WASM file. The parser module should: (1) initialize tree-sitter and load grammars lazily (load grammar on first parse of that language), (2) parse a file given its content and language, returning a tree-sitter Tree, (3) handle parse errors gracefully (partial trees are fine — tree-sitter is error-tolerant), (4) cache loaded grammars (don't re-load WASM on every file). Consideration: WASM grammar files can be large (~1-5MB each). Bundle only the most common languages; others can be loaded on demand or skipped with a warning.

title: Symbol extraction logic
description: Extract symbols (functions, classes, interfaces, types, exports, constants) from tree-sitter parse trees. For each language, define tree-sitter queries that capture: (1) function declarations + signatures, (2) class declarations + method signatures, (3) interface/type declarations, (4) export statements, (5) top-level constants/variables. The output is SymbolInfo[]: {kind, signature, line, endLine?}. Signature extraction: capture the function/class declaration line (or first N characters of it, truncated to max_signature_chars=160). Line numbers are 1-indexed (matching editor conventions). Symbol ordering: ascending by line number, then by signature string (for determinism). This is the core value of the codemap engine — it turns a 500-line file into a 20-line structural overview that helps the agent (and the reasoning model) understand what's in the file without reading it all.

title: Codemap cache (hash-keyed)
description: Cache codemap results keyed by file content hash. When a file's hash matches a cached entry, return the cached codemap without re-parsing. Cache storage options: (1) in the SQLite index (symbols table — already designed in the index schema task), (2) in-memory Map for the duration of a run (simpler, no persistence). For v1, use the SQLite symbols table as the cache: when indexing a file, extract and store symbols; when a codemap is requested, query the symbols table. This means codemap generation is effectively 'free' after indexing. The cache must be invalidated when the file content hash changes (handled by the incremental index update logic). Edge case: if tree-sitter parsing fails for a file, store an empty symbol list (don't retry on every access).

title: Progressive codemap disclosure (summary vs complete)
description:
Implement two codemap detail levels for the agent-facing codemap() tool:

SUMMARY mode (default): Return top-level symbols only (no nested methods within classes, no function-local declarations). Cap at max_symbols per file (default 200). Truncate signatures to max_signature_chars (default 160). This is compact enough to fit in agent context without blowing the budget.

COMPLETE mode: Return all symbols including nested ones (class methods, inner functions). Higher max_symbols cap. Full signatures up to the char limit. This is used when the agent wants deep detail on a specific file.

The detail level is controlled by the codemap() tool call's 'detail' parameter. The assembler also uses these modes when including codemaps in the final prompt: codemap_only files get summary mode by default, but the budget manager may shrink from complete→summary during degradation.

title: Module map generation
description: Generate a compact 'module map' for repo_info() that gives the agent a high-level overview of the codebase structure. The module map includes: (1) top-level directories with their primary languages (e.g., 'src/auth/: TypeScript, 12 files'), (2) a small set of high-level symbols per module (e.g., top 3-5 exported functions/classes), (3) total line count per module. This is NOT a full codemap dump — it's a table of contents that helps the agent decide WHERE to look. The agent then uses codemap({paths:[...]}) to drill into specific modules. Cap the module map to prevent it from being too large: max 30 modules, max 5 symbols per module. Sort modules by file count (descending) then path (ascending). This progressive disclosure is what keeps ctx scalable for large repos.
close_reason: Completed in-tree (module-map engine + tests) and validated by full bun test/build

title: Router reliability hardening: index persistence + --repo resolution + latest fallback
description: Fix router path bugs found in deep audit: persistent index path when index enabled, honor --repo for explain/manifest/open, and support latest pointer fallback when symlink unsupported.

title: Deep audit lane: tools/discovery/artifacts/index reliability hardening
close_reason: Completed deep audit lane fixes: run-store safety/reliability + ctx_final path normalization

title: Repo Scanner
description: Repository discovery, file walking, and filtering. This component owns: (1) finding the repo root (--repo flag, current dir, or git root), (2) loading and applying .gitignore rules, (3) detecting binary files via null-byte scanning + extension blocklist, (4) enforcing file size limits (--max-file-bytes, default 1.5MB), (5) applying extra ignore patterns from config. The output is a filtered file list that every downstream component (indexer, search, codemap) consumes. Must be efficient — the file walker is on the critical path for cold-start performance. Target: <500ms for repos up to 10k files.

title: Repo root detection
description: Detect the repository root directory using this priority: (1) --repo CLI flag (if provided, use as-is after validation), (2) resolve git root via 'git rev-parse --show-toplevel' from current directory, (3) fallback to current working directory. The detected root must be an absolute path and must exist. If --repo points to a non-existent or non-readable directory, exit with code 3 ('repo not found'). The root path is stored in the merged config and used by all components for relative path resolution. Edge cases: running from a subdirectory (still find git root), running outside any git repo (use cwd), --repo pointing to a symlink (resolve it).

title: Gitignore parsing and application
description: Load and apply .gitignore rules to filter the file walk. Use a gitignore-compatible library (e.g., 'ignore' npm package) that handles: nested .gitignore files, negation patterns, directory-only patterns, and the full gitignore spec. When config.repo.use_gitignore is true (default), load: (1) repo root .gitignore, (2) nested .gitignore files in subdirectories, (3) global gitignore from git config. Then layer on config.repo.ignore patterns (from .ctx/config.toml) as additional excludes. The combined ignore rules are applied during file walking to prune early (don't descend into ignored directories). Performance note: pruning directories early is critical for large repos — traversing node_modules or .git wastes time.

title: Binary file detection
description: Detect binary files to exclude them from indexing, search, and context. Two-stage detection: (1) Fast path: check file extension against a blocklist (images, archives, compiled binaries, fonts, media files — .png, .jpg, .gif, .ico, .zip, .tar, .gz, .exe, .dll, .so, .dylib, .woff, .woff2, .mp3, .mp4, .pdf, etc.). (2) Slow path: for unknown extensions, read the first 8KB and check for null bytes (0x00). If any null bytes found, classify as binary. This is the same heuristic git uses. Binary files are never included in search results, codemaps, or context — they get a manifest entry with reason 'binary'. The extension blocklist should be comprehensive but maintainable; store it as a constant array.

title: File size limit enforcement
description: Enforce --max-file-bytes (default 1,500,000 = ~1.5MB). Files exceeding this limit are excluded from full reads but may still appear in: (1) file tree listings, (2) path-only search results, (3) metadata index. The size check happens during file walking via stat(). Exception: files matching --include globs bypass the size limit (user explicitly wants them). Oversized files get a manifest entry with reason 'exceeds max_file_bytes'. Design consideration: 1.5MB is generous enough for most source files but prevents accidentally including generated files (bundle.js, compiled assets, data dumps). Users can increase this limit via config if they work with legitimately large source files.

title: Unified file walker (scan + filter pipeline)
description: The file walker is the main scanning pipeline that produces a filtered FileEntry[] for downstream consumption. It combines all filters in a single pass: (1) walk the directory tree starting from repo root, (2) apply gitignore + config ignore patterns (prune directories early), (3) skip binary files (extension check + null-byte check for unknowns), (4) skip files exceeding size limit (unless force-included), (5) skip paths matching --never-include (unconditional, cannot be overridden), (6) apply --exclude patterns, (7) for remaining files, compute FileEntry: {path (relative), size, mtime (from stat), hash (lazy — computed on first read), language (from extension), isText: true}. The walker should be async and yield results as a stream/array. Performance target: <500ms for 10k files on SSD. Must handle permission errors gracefully (skip + log warning, don't crash).

title: Polish & Performance
description: Final polish: performance benchmarking against targets (cold <8s/small, <30s/large; warm <3s/small, <10s/large), dry-run mode, --json-summary output, review mode integration, ctx init command, ctx index command, ctx templates command. These are the finishing touches that make ctx production-ready. They depend on the core pipeline being complete and tested.

title: ctx init command
description:
Implement 'ctx init' to bootstrap the .ctx/ directory in a repo:

1. Create .ctx/ directory
2. Create .ctx/config.toml with commented-out defaults (as documentation)
3. Create .ctx/templates/ directory
4. Create .ctx/runs/ directory
5. Add .ctx/runs/ to .gitignore (if .gitignore exists) — run records are local, not committed
6. Print success message with next steps

The init command is optional — ctx works without it (creates directories on first run). But it gives users a clear starting point and a config file template they can customize. Skip creation of anything that already exists (idempotent). This is a low-priority polish item.
close_reason: Completed: implemented idempotent ctx init command + tests; full test/build green

title: ctx index command
description:
Implement 'ctx index [--rebuild]':

Default (no flags): show index status — database path, total indexed files, last update time, schema version, database size.

--rebuild: drop and recreate the entire index from scratch. Useful when the index is corrupted or after major repo restructuring. Shows progress: 'Indexing file N/M...' on stderr.

This is a maintenance command. Most users never need it (incremental updates handle everything), but it's essential for troubleshooting index issues.
close_reason: Completed: implemented ctx index status/rebuild command behavior + routing tests; full test/build green

title: ctx templates command
description:
Implement 'ctx templates (list|show <name>)':

list: show all available templates with name, description, source (built-in or custom path). Format as a table:
  NAME        SOURCE      DESCRIPTION
  plan        built-in    Architecture planning with file-by-file changes
  question    built-in    Grounded code explanation
  review      built-in    Code review with severity levels
  context     built-in    Raw curated context only
  team_plan   .ctx/templates/team_plan.md   Plan with ADR and rollout gates

show <name>: display the template's full content to stdout. Useful for understanding what instructions the reasoning model will receive, or as a starting point for creating custom templates.
close_reason: Completed: implemented templates list/show CLI command + routing tests; full test/build green

title: Dry-run mode
description:
Implement --dry-run flag. When enabled:

1. Run the full pipeline EXCEPT discovery and prompt output
2. Use offline discovery only (even if LLM is configured — don't spend API calls on a dry run)
3. Instead of outputting the prompt, output a PLAN of what would happen:
   - Which files would be scanned (count + sample)
   - Which discovery backend would be used
   - Estimated token budget utilization
   - Which files would likely be included (from offline ranking)
4. Show config summary (merged config with sources of each override)

Dry-run is useful for: (1) verifying config before spending API credits, (2) checking what files ctx sees (ignore rules working?), (3) estimating token usage. It should be fast (<3s) since it skips the expensive LLM discovery step.

title: JSON summary output (--json-summary)
description:
Implement --json-summary flag. When enabled, output a machine-readable JSON summary to stderr (in addition to the prompt on stdout):

{
  "run_id": "20240115T143022-a1b2c3",
  "mode": "plan",
  "discovery_backend": "offline",
  "files_scanned": 1234,
  "files_selected": 42,
  "budget": 60000,
  "estimated_tokens": 45230,
  "degradations_applied": 3,
  "duration_ms": 2150,
  "exit_code": 0
}

This is designed for CI/CD integration and automated pipelines that need to parse ctx's behavior programmatically without reading the full prompt. The JSON is on stderr to not interfere with the prompt on stdout.
close_reason: Completed: --json-summary emits deterministic machine-readable stderr summary; tests updated; full test/build green

title: Performance benchmarking against targets
description:
Create performance benchmarks and verify against the spec targets:

Targets:
- Small repo (<500 files): cold <8s, warm <3s to reach discovery
- Large repo (<10k files): cold <30s, warm <10s to reach discovery
- Assembly: <1s
- Discovery: backend-dependent (offline <2s)

Benchmark setup:
1. Generate fixture repos at different sizes (100, 500, 2000, 10000 files)
2. Measure each pipeline phase: scan, index, discovery, budget, assembly
3. Measure cold (no cache/index) and warm (index populated) starts
4. Report as a table comparing against targets

If benchmarks miss targets, investigate and optimize:
- Scanner: parallel file walking, early directory pruning
- Index: batch SQLite operations, WAL mode
- Codemap: lazy parsing, cache hit rates
- Assembly: avoid unnecessary string concatenation

Run benchmarks in CI to catch performance regressions.

title: Testing & QA
description: Comprehensive test suite ensuring correctness, determinism, and safety. Includes: golden prompt snapshot tests (same repo+task → same output), budget enforcement tests (overbudget → predictable degradation), redaction tests (secrets replaced, line numbers preserved), binary/large-file exclusion tests, index incremental update tests, git diff correctness tests, tool protocol contract tests (ctx_tool/ctx_result/ctx_final parsing and validation), and end-to-end integration tests. The test suite IS the specification — if a behavior isn't tested, it's not guaranteed.

title: Golden prompt snapshot tests
description:
Create snapshot tests that verify ctx produces identical output given the same inputs. Setup:

1. Create a small fixture repo (5-10 files, multiple languages, realistic structure)
2. Run ctx with a fixed task and fixed config (offline discovery, deterministic)
3. Save the output as a golden snapshot
4. On each test run, regenerate and compare against snapshot
5. If output differs: test FAILS (the determinism guarantee is broken)

Test cases:
- Simple task on small repo → snapshot A
- Same task, different budget (trigger degradation) → snapshot B
- Review mode with git diff → snapshot C
- Question mode → snapshot D
- Context mode → snapshot E

These tests are the MOST IMPORTANT tests in the project. They prove the core guarantee: same inputs → same outputs. If a code change breaks a snapshot, we must either fix the code or deliberately update the snapshot with a justification. Run these tests in CI on every commit.

title: Budget enforcement and degradation tests
description:
Test the deterministic degradation ladder under various budget constraints:

1. Test: 50 files selected, budget 60000 → no degradation needed → verify all kept
2. Test: 50 files selected, budget 10000 → heavy degradation → verify ladder order:
   - lowest-priority full files degraded to slices first
   - then slices to codemap_only
   - then codemap_only dropped
   - correct order throughout
3. Test: exactly at budget boundary → no degradation (verify no off-by-one)
4. Test: single huge file exceeding budget alone → degraded to slices → still too big → codemap_only → fits
5. Test: --fail-on-overbudget with overbudget → verify exit code 6
6. Test: degradation report matches actual degradation actions
7. Test: priority ordering is deterministic (same files, same task → same degradation order every time)

Each test should verify both the final selection AND the degradation log (which files were degraded, in what order, by how much).
close_reason: Completed: added deterministic budget/degradation QA coverage and report-delta assertions

title: Redaction correctness tests
description:
Test the redaction engine thoroughly:

1. Test: AWS key (AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE) → replaced with ‹REDACTED:aws_key›
2. Test: GitHub token (ghp_xxxx...) → replaced with ‹REDACTED:github_token›
3. Test: JWT (eyJhbGciOi...) → replaced with ‹REDACTED:jwt›
4. Test: Private key block (-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----) → replaced
5. Test: Connection string with password → password portion redacted
6. Test: Custom pattern (from config) → detected and redacted
7. Test: Redaction preserves line count (critical invariant!)
8. Test: Redaction preserves surrounding context (only sensitive substring replaced)
9. Test: Multiple secrets on one line → all replaced independently
10. Test: False positive resistance — normal code (base64 data URLs, hash constants) should NOT be redacted
11. Test: --redact off → no redaction applied
12. Test: Redaction tracking (count and categories reported correctly)

Line number preservation is the hardest invariant — verify with a file that has secrets on known lines and check that line numbers in slices still match after redaction.

title: Binary and large-file exclusion tests
description:
Test that binary and oversized files are properly excluded:

1. Test: PNG file → detected as binary by extension → excluded
2. Test: File with null bytes but .txt extension → detected as binary by content → excluded
3. Test: File exactly at size limit (1,500,000 bytes) → included
4. Test: File 1 byte over size limit → excluded
5. Test: Large file with --include forcing it → included despite size limit
6. Test: Binary file cannot be force-included (even with --include, binary = binary)
7. Test: Excluded files appear in manifest with correct reason ('binary' or 'exceeds max_file_bytes')
8. Test: Scanner performance doesn't degrade with many binary files (should be filtered early)

Create test fixtures: small PNG file, a text file with a null byte injected, files at/near the size boundary.

title: Index incremental update tests
description:
Test that the index correctly handles incremental updates:

1. Test: First index of 10 files → all 10 indexed
2. Test: Change 1 file's content (update mtime) → only that file re-indexed, others unchanged
3. Test: Add a new file → new file indexed, existing unchanged
4. Test: Delete a file → removed from index, others unchanged
5. Test: No changes → zero files re-indexed (fast path)
6. Test: Change file mtime but not content → mtime updated but no symbol re-extraction needed
7. Test: Schema version change → full rebuild triggered
8. Test: Concurrent index access doesn't corrupt (SQLite WAL mode)
9. Test: Index with --no-index flag → no database created/accessed

Measure timing: re-indexing 1 changed file in a 1000-file index should take <100ms.

title: Git diff correctness tests
description:
Test git diff integration:

1. Test: uncommitted changes → correct diff output
2. Test: staged changes → only staged changes shown
3. Test: compare:main → shows changes since diverging from main
4. Test: back:3 → shows last 3 commits of changes
5. Test: Patch token capping → large diff truncated at hunk boundaries
6. Test: Diff with renames → correctly identified
7. Test: Diff with binary files → noted as binary, content not included
8. Test: Diff mode 'off' → no diff in output
9. Test: git-max-files cap → only top N files included
10. Test: Not a git repo → diff operations gracefully return empty results

Use a temp git repo fixture with known commits for deterministic test inputs.

title: Tool protocol contract tests
description:
Test the tool protocol parsing and validation:

1. Test: Valid ctx_tool block parsed correctly (id, tool, args extracted)
2. Test: Multiple ctx_tool blocks in one response → all parsed in order
3. Test: Malformed JSON in ctx_tool → error returned, other blocks still parsed
4. Test: Missing 'id' field → validation error
5. Test: Unknown tool name → INVALID_TOOL error in ctx_result
6. Test: Invalid args for known tool → INVALID_ARGS error with explanation
7. Test: ctx_result formatting matches spec (success and error cases)
8. Test: Valid ctx_final parsed and validated correctly
9. Test: ctx_final with missing required fields → validation error returned
10. Test: ctx_final with extra unknown fields → accepted (lenient on extras)
11. Test: ctx_final with invalid path in selection → validation error
12. Test: ctx_tool blocks mixed with plain text → only blocks extracted, text ignored

These tests validate the contract between ctx and any discovery agent. If these pass, any agent that follows the protocol will work correctly.

title: End-to-end integration test
description:
Full pipeline integration test that runs ctx end-to-end:

1. Create a realistic fixture repo (TypeScript project, 20+ files, package.json, tsconfig, tests, routes)
2. Run ctx offline: 'ctx --no-llm --budget 30000 "Add user authentication"'
3. Verify: exit code 0, stdout is valid prompt, stderr has progress info
4. Verify: prompt contains expected sections (task, tree, codemaps, files)
5. Verify: prompt stays within budget
6. Verify: run record created in .ctx/runs/
7. Run ctx explain last → verify output is readable and correct
8. Run ctx manifest last --json → verify valid JSON schema
9. Run with --copy → verify clipboard attempt (mock clipboard command)
10. Run with -o output.md → verify file written
11. Run with invalid args → exit code 2
12. Run in non-git directory → still works (git features disabled)

This test proves the entire system works as specified. It should run in CI and catch integration issues between components.

title: Project Scaffolding & Build Setup
description: Bootstrap the ctx TypeScript/bun project. This is the absolute foundation — nothing else can start until we have a runnable project with test framework, build config, and basic directory structure. We use bun exclusively (per AGENTS.md), TypeScript with strict mode, and bun:test for testing. The project produces a single CLI binary 'ctx'.

title: Initialize bun project with TypeScript strict mode
description: Create the project root with: package.json (name: ctx, type: module, bin: {ctx: ./dist/ctx.js}), tsconfig.json (strict: true, target: ESNext, module: ESNext, moduleResolution: bundler, outDir: dist), .gitignore (node_modules, dist, .ctx/runs, *.db), and src/index.ts entrypoint. Use bun exclusively — no npm/yarn/pnpm. The package.json should include scripts for build (bun build src/index.ts --outdir dist --target bun), test (bun test), and dev (bun run src/index.ts). This is the absolute first step — everything else depends on being able to run and test code.

title: Test framework setup with bun:test
description: Configure bun:test as the test framework. Create a test/ directory with a smoke test that imports from src/ and runs. Verify that 'bun test' works. Add test script to package.json. We use bun's built-in test runner exclusively — no jest, vitest, or other frameworks. Tests should follow the pattern test/<component>/<feature>.test.ts. This must work before any other development proceeds, because we want TDD from the start.

title: Source directory structure and module layout
description: Create the source directory structure that maps to the component architecture: src/cli/ (CLI frontend), src/config/ (config manager), src/scanner/ (repo scanner), src/index-manager/ (SQLite index — 'index' is a reserved word so use index-manager), src/search/ (search engine), src/codemap/ (codemap engine), src/privacy/ (redaction), src/git/ (git integration), src/selection/ (selection + budget), src/tools/ (tool host + agent-facing tools), src/discovery/ (discovery runner), src/prompt/ (prompt assembler), src/artifacts/ (run records), src/types/ (shared types), src/utils/ (shared utilities). Each directory gets an index.ts barrel export. This establishes the module boundaries early.
close_reason: Completed: created module directory layout with per-module barrel index.ts files
